text.title: Niche Evaluation, Validation Criteria, and JTBD
text.summary: 50-point niche scoring matrix, validation criteria (frequency/intensity/WTP/search/competition), and Jobs-to-be-Done statement template with measurability rules.

rule.statement: Score each of five criteria 1-10, require a one-line justification AND a cited source URL for market-size and competition columns before accepting the score.
rule.rationale: A 50/50 niche score with zero primary research is worse than no score — it legitimizes guesses. Citations force the agent to ground numbers in real evidence.

list.item: Competition (20%): 1-3 = red ocean (saturated), 4-6 = moderate, 7-10 = blue ocean (new)
Barriers to entry (20%): 1-3 = high (capital, regulatory), 4-6 = medium, 7-10 = low (can start tomorrow)
Personal fit (15%): 1-3 = no relevant skills, 4-6 = some skills, 7-10 = perfect match
20-29 points: proceed with caution, consider pivots
<20 points: consider other ideas or major pivots

rule.statement: Apply all five validation criteria (frequency, intensity, WTP, search volume, competition) before recommending pursuit; the presence of competitors is a green flag, not a red flag — it confirms the problem exists.
rule.rationale: No competitors usually means no market, not a blue ocean. Absence of search volume means people are not actively looking for a solution. Both are failure signals.

list.item: Frequency: must be weekly or more — "How often do you face this?" Monthly or less fails.
Intensity: 7+ out of 10 — "How painful is this (1-10)?" Below 7 rarely creates urgency to pay.
Willingness to pay: must be yes — "Would you pay to solve this?" and a dollar figure, not just yes/no.
Search volume: must exist — Google Trends, keyword volume. People looking for solutions confirms demand.
Competition: must exist — someone trying to solve it validates the problem; gaps in existing solutions are the opportunity.

rule.statement: Write the functional JTBD statement first using the "When/I want/So I can" template with a verifiable outcome — reject any outcome phrased as an adjective ("feel empowered") and replace with a measurable verb ("invoice sent in <90s, paid within 14 days").
rule.rationale: JTBD statements drift into wishful copywriting when outcomes are unmeasurable. Verifiable outcomes anchor product decisions and can be tested in user research.

example.note: Functional JTBD: "When I finish client work, I want to quickly generate and send professional invoices, so I can get paid within 14 days without a follow-up call."
Wishful JTBD: "When I finish client work, I want to feel professional and empowered, so I can build my reputation." — no measurable outcome, cannot be tested.

list.item: Functional jobs: what tasks are they trying to accomplish? ("Send invoice to client")
Emotional jobs: how do they want to feel? Derive from functional, do not invent. ("Feel confident the payment will arrive")
Social jobs: how do they want to be perceived? Derive from functional. ("Be seen as reliable by clients")
